content.text: Why Reliability Work Needs Its Own Roadmap

Feature work competes with reliability work for engineering capacity. Without an explicit reliability roadmap, reliability work always loses — it is invisible, deferred until an incident forces it, and then done reactively under time pressure.

A reliability roadmap makes the tradeoffs visible. It answers: given our current reliability posture and the feature launches planned for the next quarter, what reliability investments do we need to make, and in what order?

attrs.alt: Reliability work prioritisation matrix: impact vs urgency quadrants

content.text: Input Sources for the Roadmap
A reliability roadmap is built from four input sources, each covering a different failure horizon:
Input Source
What It Contributes
Cadence
Monthly reliability review
Past incidents, current SLO posture, open action items, toil trends
Monthly
Chaos experiment findings
Known gaps in recovery paths, unvalidated failure modes
Per-experiment + quarterly coverage review
Capacity forecasts
Upcoming headroom thresholds, growth-driven reliability risks
Monthly / quarterly
Feature roadmap
Planned launches and their reliability requirements (new endpoints, higher token usage, new models)
Each planning cycle
Postmortem action items
Root cause fixes that were logged but not yet scheduled
Continuous; reviewed monthly
A reliability roadmap that is not connected to the feature roadmap is not a real roadmap — it is a wishlist. Block time for reliability work in the same sprint planning where features are scheduled.
Scoring Reliability Work Items

Prioritisation requires a score for each item. The two axes are impact (how much reliability improves if this is done) and urgency (how soon the problem causes a user-visible failure if not addressed).

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal


@dataclass
class ReliabilityWorkItem:
    id: str
    title: str
    source: str                    # "postmortem", "chaos", "toil", "roadmap_risk"
    impact_score: int              # 1–5: estimated SLO-minutes-saved per month if done
    urgency_score: int             # 1–5: months until failure if not done (5=imminent)
    effort_weeks: float            # engineering weeks
    owner: str

    @property
    def priority_score(self) -> float:
        return (self.impact_score * self.urgency_score) / max(self.effort_weeks, 0.5)

    @property
    def quadrant(self) -> Literal["Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4"]:
        high_impact = self.impact_score >= 3
        high_urgency = self.urgency_score >= 3
        if high_impact and high_urgency:
            return "Q1"
        if not high_impact and high_urgency:
            return "Q2"
        if high_impact and not high_urgency:
            return "Q3"
        return "Q4"


BACKLOG = [
    ReliabilityWorkItem("REL-001", "Add circuit breaker for Claude API calls",
                        "chaos", 5, 4, 1.0, "@bob"),
    ReliabilityWorkItem("REL-002", "Request 30% quota increase before Q4 launch",
                        "roadmap_risk", 4, 5, 0.5, "@alice"),
    ReliabilityWorkItem("REL-003", "Build multi-region failover for claude-worker",
                        "postmortem", 5, 2, 8.0, "@carol"),
    ReliabilityWorkItem("REL-004", "Reduce p99 alert window from 5 min to 1 min",
                        "toil", 2, 4, 0.25, "@alice"),
    ReliabilityWorkItem("REL-005", "Implement prompt template promotion pipeline",
                        "toil", 3, 2, 3.0, "@dave"),
]

for item in sorted(BACKLOG, key=lambda x: x.priority_score, reverse=True):
    print(f"[{item.quadrant}] {item.id} {item.title} (score={item.priority_score:.1f})")
# Output (approximate):
# [Q1] REL-002 Request 30% quota increase before Q4 launch (score=40.0)
# [Q1] REL-001 Add circuit breaker for Claude API calls (score=20.0)
# [Q2] REL-004 Reduce p99 alert window from 5 min to 1 min (score=32.0)
# [Q3] REL-005 Implement prompt template promotion pipeline (score=2.0)
# [Q3] REL-003 Build multi-region failover (score=1.25)
Balancing Reliability and Feature Work

The SRE industry convention is a 50% cap on operational/toil work, with the remaining 50% available for engineering work (reliability improvements, feature support, tooling). In practice, you must negotiate the split explicitly each quarter.
Scenario
Appropriate Split
Signal
Error budget > 50% remaining, no active incidents, healthy toil rate
70% features, 30% reliability
System is healthy; invest in velocity
Error budget 20–50% remaining, some recurring incidents
50% features, 50% reliability
Balanced; catch up on open action items
Error budget < 20% remaining or P0 in last 30 days
30% features, 70% reliability
Reliability debt is accumulating; prioritise fixes
Error budget exhausted
0% features, 100% reliability
Release freeze; no new feature deployments until budget recovers

These splits are guidelines for negotiation, not hard rules. A critical security fix might ship at any budget level. The splits give teams a principled starting position rather than an argument from intuition.
Quarterly Reliability Planning Session

Monthly reviews handle the tactical work (incidents, toil, open items). Quarterly planning handles strategic investments: architectural improvements, new monitoring capabilities, and cross-service reliability dependencies.
Review the full reliability backlog. Score every open item. Remove items that are no longer relevant.
Identify items that must land before any planned feature launches — these are non-negotiable and go into the sprint plan first.
Group the remaining Q1 and Q3 items into themes (e.g., 'quota resilience', 'observability completeness'). A theme that spans several items can be pitched as an investment project.
Negotiate capacity with the feature team: present the projected error budget trajectory if the top-3 reliability items are not done.
Set quarterly OKRs for reliability: 'reduce MTTR P0 from 35 min to 20 min', 'validate three new chaos experiments', 'reduce alert noise by 40%'.
Projecting Error Budget Trajectory
Communicating Reliability Work to Stakeholders
Engineering managers and product owners respond to business risk, not technical metrics. Translate reliability roadmap items into business terms when requesting capacity.
Technical Framing
Business Framing
Error budget is at 20%, p99 latency is degrading
If current trends continue, we will breach our SLA in 6 weeks, triggering penalty clauses in enterprise contracts
We need a circuit breaker before the Q4 launch
Without a circuit breaker, a Claude API disruption during the Q4 launch will cause 100% failure instead of 5% degradation — the feature will be unusable
We need to reduce alert noise by 40%
On-call engineers are investigating 7 false-positive alerts per week; that is 1 person-day per week not spent on engineering work
Multi-region failover project (8 weeks)
Currently, a US-East-1 outage takes down the service for all users. After this project, failover happens in under 5 minutes with no manual intervention
Example Quarterly Reliability Roadmap
The following table shows what a quarterly roadmap looks like for a team running a Claude-based application with two planned feature launches.
Quarter
Item
Quadrant
Effort
Driver
Q3 Week 1–2
Request 30% quota increase (REL-002)
Q1
Q4 launch risk
Q3 Week 2–3
Add circuit breaker (REL-001)
Q1
Chaos finding + Q4 launch risk
Q3 Week 3–4
Reduce p99 alert window (REL-004)
Q2
Toil reduction
Q3 Week 4–6
Prompt template promotion pipeline (REL-005)
Q3
Recurring toil
Q4 Week 1–8
Multi-region failover (REL-003)
Q3
DR requirement

Q1 items go first regardless of effort. A 0.5-week quota increase request that prevents a launch-day outage is more valuable than 8 weeks on a long-term architectural project, even though both are on the roadmap.
Tracking Roadmap Progress
A reliability roadmap is only useful if progress is tracked. Without tracking, items silently fall off the list because engineers shift to urgent operational work. Use three tracking artefacts:
A GitHub project board (or Jira board) with columns: Backlog, This Quarter, In Progress, Done. Every roadmap item lives here as an issue.
A weekly 15-minute sync between the SRE lead and engineering manager to review the board. Any item blocked for more than two weeks gets escalated.
A monthly reliability review section: 'Roadmap Progress'. List each item scheduled for the current quarter and its current status.
Add the reliability-roadmap label and the quarter label (Q3-2026, Q4-2026) to every item when it is created. This makes the script above work without any configuration changes.
Reliability OKRs and How to Set Them

Quarterly reliability OKRs translate the roadmap into measurable commitments. They give engineering managers a target to hold the team accountable to, and give engineers clarity about what success looks like.

A reliability OKR follows the same structure as any OKR: an aspirational Objective and 2–4 measurable Key Results. The difference is that the Key Results are derived directly from the roadmap items and backlog metrics.
Objective
Key Result
Source
Improve Claude worker resilience for Q4 launch
Circuit breaker deployed and validated by chaos experiment by end of Q3 Week 3
REL-001 from roadmap
Improve Claude worker resilience for Q4 launch
Quota increase approved and active 4 weeks before Q4 launch date
REL-002 from roadmap
Reduce on-call toil by 30%
Total toil hours per month drops from 12 h to 8.4 h by end of quarter
Toil audit trend
Reduce on-call toil by 30%
Stale job purge automated; zero manual queue purges in last 4 weeks of quarter
REL-004 from toil backlog
Improve detection speed
p99 alert window reduced from 5 min to 1 min; MTTR P2 drops from 25 min to 18 min
Alert noise reduction item
Common Mistakes When Setting Reliability OKRs
Activity-based Key Results instead of outcome-based: 'Complete multi-region failover project' is an activity. 'Reduce RTO from 30 min to 5 min for claude-worker' is an outcome.
Setting OKRs without committing capacity: a reliability OKR with no sprint allocation is a wishlist. Block engineering weeks before publishing the OKR.
No baseline measurement: 'Reduce MTTR by 20%' requires knowing current MTTR. Measure first; then set the target.

Quarterly OKRs for work that should be weekly: reducing alert noise by 40% is a 2-week project, not a quarterly OKR. Use the monthly reliability review for fast items; save OKRs for work that takes most of the quarter.
Handling Unplanned Reliability Work
Reliability roadmaps are disrupted by incidents, urgent toil spikes, and emergency feature launches. Rather than abandoning the roadmap when this happens, use a structured trade-off process.
Disruption Type
Impact on Roadmap
Response
P0 incident postmortem produces new Q1 items
Displaces 1–2 sprint slots from planned items
Defer lowest-priority Q3 item; reschedule to next quarter
Emergency feature launch requires infra prep
Consumes reliability capacity for 1 week
Negotiate: either delay the launch 1 week, or defer a reliability OKR and document the risk
Error budget exhausts mid-quarter
All feature work halts per release freeze policy
Focus 100% on the highest-impact reliability item; resume feature work when budget recovers above 20%
Toil spike (new failure mode requires manual intervention weekly)
Eats capacity without producing roadmap progress
Add automation item to Q1 backlog; deprioritise a Q3 item

Every roadmap trade-off must be documented. If you defer REL-003 to accommodate an emergency launch, record the decision in the roadmap issue with a note explaining why. This prevents the item from being forgotten and creates an audit trail for post-quarter retrospectives.
Connecting the Roadmap to Anthropic API Dependency Risk

Claude-powered services have a reliability dependency that most internal services do not: the Anthropic API. Changes to the API — new model versions, pricing tier changes, rate limit adjustments, deprecations — can require reliability investments that were not in your original roadmap.
Build a standing agenda item in the quarterly reliability planning session for 'Anthropic dependency review.' Check three things:

Any model deprecations announced in the Anthropic changelog that affect your default model. A model deprecation requires testing the replacement and updating rate limit calculations (different models have different token limits and pricing).
Rate limit tier changes. If Anthropic adjusts default rate limits for your tier, your capacity model and quota increase requests need to be recalibrated.

New API features (e.g., prompt caching, batch API, tool use changes) that could reduce your reliability risk or token consumption. These are positive reliability investments — scheduling a migration can be a roadmap item.

# model_migration_checklist.py
# Use when migrating from one Claude model to another (e.g., claude-sonnet-5 → new model)
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal


@dataclass
class ModelMigrationTask:
    task: str
    owner: str
    effort: Literal["hours", "days", "weeks"]
    status: Literal["pending", "in_progress", "done"]


MIGRATION_CHECKLIST = [
    ModelMigrationTask("Audit all hardcoded model IDs in codebase", "@alice", "hours", "pending"),
    ModelMigrationTask("Update rate limit / token budget calculations for new model context window", "@bob", "hours", "pending"),
    ModelMigrationTask("Run integration tests against new model in staging", "@carol", "days", "pending"),
    ModelMigrationTask("Compare output quality on 100 representative prompts", "@dave", "days", "pending"),
    ModelMigrationTask("Update capacity forecast: new model pricing may change monthly token cost", "@alice", "hours", "pending"),
    ModelMigrationTask("Update chaos experiment specs if model-specific behaviour changed", "@bob", "hours", "pending"),
    ModelMigrationTask("Staged rollout: canary 5% → 25% → 100% with error rate gate at each step", "@carol", "days", "pending"),
    ModelMigrationTask("Update runbooks and dashboards with new model ID", "@alice", "hours", "pending"),
]

for task in MIGRATION_CHECKLIST:
    status_symbol = {"pending": "[ ]", "in_progress": "[~]", "done": "[x]"}[task.status]
    print(f"{status_symbol} [{task.effort}] {task.task} — {task.owner}")

Schedule a model migration as a Q3 item (high impact, low urgency) in your roadmap if you are still on a model that has a published deprecation date more than 90 days away. Do not wait until the deprecation is 30 days away — rushed migrations produce reliability regressions.
Summary
A reliability roadmap is the bridge between operational data (incidents, toil, chaos findings) and planned engineering investment. Without it, reliability work is ad hoc and reactive.
Build the roadmap from four inputs: monthly review action items, chaos findings, capacity forecasts, and feature launch plans.
Score each item by impact × urgency ÷ effort to produce a priority score and assign items to the four quadrants.
Q1 items (high impact, high urgency) go into the current sprint regardless of competing feature work.
Negotiate capacity using the feature-vs-reliability split table: less budget remaining → more reliability investment.
Run a quarterly planning session to score the full backlog, identify launch-blocking items, and set OKRs.
Project the error budget trajectory when requesting stakeholder buy-in; business-frame all technical asks.
